tagline: Contract-follower: state the outcome once, cleanly, and let it choose the path — less instruction beats more, and contradictions hurt.
effort_line: Set `reasoning_effort` explicitly — none|low|medium|high|xhigh|max (5.6 default = medium) — and `text.verbosity` (low|medium|high). Then A/B one effort level LOWER on your evals; 5.6 is calibrated to match quality at lower cost.
idioms: Fence discrete instruction blocks in XML-style tags (<constraints>, <tool_usage_rules>, <output_spec>) with Markdown prose/bullets INSIDE — this is how OpenAI's own GPT-5 guides are written and it out-steers walls of Markdown headers.

For any structured output use strict Structured Outputs (Responses API text.format={type:'json_schema',name,strict:true,schema}; additionalProperties:false, every field required, optionals as ['string','null'], 'set null rather than guessing') and check the refusal field.
guardrail_extra: Don't over-prompt: remove restated rules, redundant examples, and verbose tool descriptions (OpenAI's coding-agent evals show ~10-15% gain, -41-66% tokens — directional, validate on your tasks).
verify_note: Before shipping a large prompt, metaprompt it (ask GPT-5.6 to find internal conflicts/redundancy) or run the Playground Prompt Optimizer.
notes: GPT-5.6 'Sol' (GA 2026-07-09; tiers Sol/Sol Ultra/Terra/Luna) is a prompt-sensitive contract-follower: its instability comes from consistency problems, not length. Front-load Role/Goal/Constraints, keep stable content first for prompt caching, and dedupe. Prefer the Responses API; Chat Completions still uses response_format for structured output. Reasoning is opt-in — don't assume it. XML-fenced sections are documented in the 5.2 guide; the 'conflicting rules > missing detail' warning is on the 5.6 page; the ChatGPT Instant-vs-Thinking adaptive routing is a product-announcement detail, not developer-API behavior. Cite the model-specific reasoning page (none|low|medium|high|xhigh|max), not the generic family guide (which lists minimal, omits max).
